Two Sum.explanation: Use a hash map to store the value and its index as you iterate through the array. For each element, check if the complement (target - current value) exists in the hash map. This gives an O(n) time complexity.
Two Sum.description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target.

Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock.explanation: Keep track of the minimum price seen so far and the maximum profit. Iterate through the prices, updating the minimum price and calculating the potential profit at each step. O(n) time complexity.
Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock.description: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the ith day. Return the maximum profit you can achieve from this transaction.

Contains Duplicate.explanation: Use a hash set to keep track of seen elements. If an element is already in the set, return True. If the loop finishes, return False. O(n) time and O(n) space.
Contains Duplicate.implementation: class Solution:\n    def containsDuplicate(self, nums: List[int]) -> bool:\n        return len(set(nums)) != len(nums)
Contains Duplicate.description: Given an integer array nums, return true if any value appears at least twice in the array, and return false if every element is distinct.

Product of Array Except Self.explanation: Calculate the prefix products and suffix products for each element. You can optimize space by storing the prefix products in the result array and computing the suffix products on the fly. O(n) time.
Product of Array Except Self.description: Given an integer array nums, return an array answer such that answer[i] is equal to the product of all the elements of nums except nums[i].

Maximum Subarray.explanation: Use Kadane's Algorithm. Keep a running sum of the current subarray. If the running sum becomes negative, reset it to 0. Update the maximum sum seen so far at each step.
Maximum Subarray.description: Given an integer array nums, find the contiguous subarray (containing at least one number) which has the largest sum and return its sum.

Maximum Product Subarray.explanation: Keep track of both the maximum and minimum product ending at the current position, because a negative number multiplied by a minimum (negative) product can become the new maximum.
Maximum Product Subarray.description: Given an integer array nums, find a contiguous non-empty subarray within the array that has the largest product, and return the product.

Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array.explanation: Use binary search. If the middle element is greater than the rightmost element, the minimum is to the right. Otherwise, it is to the left (including mid).
Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array.description: Suppose an array of length n sorted in ascending order is rotated between 1 and n times. Given the sorted rotated array nums of unique elements, return the minimum element of this array.

Search in Rotated Sorted Array.explanation: Use binary search. Determine which half of the array is sorted. Check if the target lies within the sorted half. If it does, narrow the search to that half; otherwise, search the other half.
Search in Rotated Sorted Array.description: Given the array nums after the possible rotation and an integer target, return the index of target if it is in nums, or -1 if it is not in nums.

3Sum.explanation: Sort the array. Iterate through the array, and for each element, use two pointers (left and right) to find two other elements that sum to the negative of the current element. Skip duplicates to avoid duplicate triplets.
3Sum.description: Given an integer array nums, return all the triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i != j, i != k, and j != k, and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0.

Container With Most Water.explanation: Use two pointers, one at the beginning and one at the end. The area is limited by the shorter line. Move the pointer pointing to the shorter line inward to potentially find a taller line and a larger area.
Container With Most Water.description: You are given an integer array height of length n. There are n vertical lines drawn such that the two endpoints of the ith line are (i, 0) and (i, height[i]). Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container, such that the container contains the most water.

Sum of Two Integers.explanation: Use bit manipulation. The XOR operator (^) performs addition without carry. The AND operator (&) followed by a left shift (<< 1) calculates the carry. Repeat until there is no carry.
Sum of Two Integers.description: Given two integers a and b, return the sum of the two integers without using the operators + and -.

Number of 1 Bits.explanation: Use the bitwise AND operation n & (n - 1) to clear the lowest set bit. Count how many times you can do this until the number becomes 0.
Number of 1 Bits.description: Write a function that takes an unsigned integer and returns the number of '1' bits it has (also known as the Hamming weight).

Counting Bits.explanation: Use dynamic programming. The number of 1s in binary representation of i is equal to the number of 1s in i // 2 plus 1 if i is odd.
Counting Bits.description: Given an integer n, return an array ans of length n + 1 such that for each i (0 <= i <= n), ans[i] is the number of 1's in the binary representation of i.

Missing Number.explanation: Calculate the expected sum of numbers from 0 to n using the formula n * (n + 1) / 2. Subtract the actual sum of the array from the expected sum to find the missing number.
Missing Number.description: Given an array nums containing n distinct numbers in the range [0, n], return the only number in the range that is missing from the array.

Reverse Bits.explanation: Iterate 32 times. In each iteration, extract the rightmost bit of n, shift the result to the left, and add the extracted bit. Then shift n to the right.
Reverse Bits.description: Reverse bits of a given 32 bits unsigned integer.

Climbing Stairs.explanation: This is a Fibonacci sequence problem. The number of ways to reach step n is the sum of ways to reach step n-1 and step n-2. Use dynamic programming to store previous results.
Climbing Stairs.description: You are climbing a staircase. It takes n steps to reach the top. Each time you can either climb 1 or 2 steps. In how many distinct ways can you climb to the top?

Coin Change.explanation: Use dynamic programming. Create an array dp where dp[i] is the minimum coins needed for amount i. Initialize with infinity. For each amount, try all coins and update dp[i] = min(dp[i], 1 + dp[i - coin]).
Coin Change.description: You are given an integer array coins representing coins of different denominations and an integer amount representing a total amount of money. Return the fewest number of coins that you need to make up that amount.

Longest Increasing Subsequence.explanation: Use dynamic programming. dp[i] represents the length of the LIS ending at index i. For each element, check all previous elements. If a previous element is smaller, update dp[i] = max(dp[i], 1 + dp[prev]).
Longest Increasing Subsequence.description: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.

Longest Common Subsequence.explanation: Use a 2D DP table. If characters match, dp[i][j] = 1 + dp[i+1][j+1]. If they don't match, take the max of skipping a character from either string: max(dp[i+1][j], dp[i][j+1]).
Longest Common Subsequence.description: Given two strings text1 and text2, return the length of their longest common subsequence. If there is no common subsequence, return 0.

Word Break.explanation: Use dynamic programming. dp[i] is True if the substring up to index i can be segmented. For each index, check if any word in the dictionary matches the substring starting at i.
Word Break.description: Given a string s and a dictionary of strings wordDict, return true if s can be segmented into a space-separated sequence of one or more dictionary words.

Combination Sum.explanation: Use backtracking. At each step, you can either include the current candidate (and stay on the same index since reuse is allowed) or skip to the next candidate. Base cases are when target == 0 (success) or target < 0 (fail).
Combination Sum.description: Given an array of distinct integers candidates and a target integer target, return a list of all unique combinations of candidates where the chosen numbers sum to target.

House Robber.explanation: Use dynamic programming. For each house, you can either rob it (adding its value to the max loot from two houses ago) or skip it (keeping the max loot from the previous house).
House Robber.description: You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has a certain amount of money stashed, the only constraint stopping you from robbing each of them is that adjacent houses have security systems connected and it will automatically contact the police if two adjacent houses were broken into on the same night.

House Robber II.explanation: Since the houses are in a circle, you cannot rob both the first and last house. Run the standard House Robber algorithm twice: once excluding the first house, and once excluding the last house. Return the maximum.
House Robber II.description: You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has a certain amount of money stashed. All houses at this place are arranged in a circle. That means the first house is the neighbor of the last one.

Decode Ways.explanation: Use dynamic programming. dp[i] represents the number of ways to decode the string up to index i. Check if the single digit is valid (1-9) and if the two digits are valid (10-26).
Decode Ways.description: A message containing letters from A-Z can be encoded into numbers using the following mapping: 'A' -> "1", 'B' -> "2", ... 'Z' -> "26". Given a string s containing only digits, return the number of ways to decode it.

Unique Paths.explanation: Use dynamic programming. The number of ways to reach a cell is the sum of the ways to reach the cell above it and the cell to its left. Initialize the top row and left column to 1.
Unique Paths.description: There is a robot on an m x n grid. The robot is initially located at the top-left corner (i.e., grid[0][0]). The robot tries to move to the bottom-right corner (i.e., grid[m - 1][n - 1]). The robot can only move either down or right at any point in time.

Jump Game.explanation: Use a greedy approach. Keep track of the 'goal' index, initially the last index. Iterate backwards. If the current index plus its jump length can reach or exceed the goal, update the goal to the current index. If goal reaches 0, return True.
Jump Game.description: You are given an integer array nums. You are initially positioned at the array's first index, and each element in the array represents your maximum jump length at that position. Return true if you can reach the last index, or false otherwise.

Clone Graph.explanation: Use Depth First Search (DFS) and a hash map to keep track of already cloned nodes. For each node, create a copy, add it to the map, and recursively clone its neighbors.
Clone Graph.description: Given a reference of a node in a connected undirected graph. Return a deep copy (clone) of the graph.

Course Schedule.explanation: This is a cycle detection problem in a directed graph. Use DFS to detect cycles. Keep track of nodes currently in the recursion stack (visiting). If you encounter a node already in the visiting set, a cycle exists.
Course Schedule.description: There are a total of numCourses courses you have to take, labeled from 0 to numCourses - 1. You are given an array prerequisites where prerequisites[i] = [ai, bi] indicates that you must take course bi first if you want to take course ai. Return true if you can finish all courses. Otherwise, return false.

Pacific Atlantic Water Flow.explanation: Start DFS/BFS from the ocean borders and work inwards, finding all cells that can reach the Pacific and all cells that can reach the Atlantic. The intersection of these two sets is the answer.
Pacific Atlantic Water Flow.description: There is an m x n rectangular island that borders both the Pacific Ocean and Atlantic Ocean. The Pacific Ocean touches the island's left and top edges, and the Atlantic Ocean touches the island's right and bottom edges. Return a list of grid coordinates result where result[i] = [ri, ci] denotes that rain water can flow from cell (ri, ci) to both the Pacific and Atlantic oceans.

Number of Islands.explanation: Iterate through the grid. When you find a '1' (land), increment the island count and use DFS or BFS to mark all connected land as visited (e.g., by changing '1' to '0').
Number of Islands.description: Given an m x n 2D binary grid grid which represents a map of '1's (land) and '0's (water), return the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and is formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically.

Longest Consecutive Sequence.explanation: Convert the array to a hash set for O(1) lookups. For each number, check if it's the start of a sequence (i.e., num - 1 is not in the set). If it is, count the length of the sequence by repeatedly checking for num + 1.
Longest Consecutive Sequence.description: Given an unsorted array of integers nums, return the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence. You must write an algorithm that runs in O(n) time.

Alien Dictionary.explanation: Build a directed graph where edges represent character precedence. Perform a topological sort using DFS. If a cycle is detected, return an empty string.
Alien Dictionary.description: There is a new alien language that uses the English alphabet. However, the order among the letters is unknown to you. You are given a list of strings words from the alien language's dictionary, where the strings in words are sorted lexicographically by the rules of this new language. Return a string of the unique letters in the new alien language sorted in lexicographically increasing order by the new language's rules.

Graph Valid Tree.explanation: A valid tree must have exactly n - 1 edges and all nodes must be connected. Use DFS or Union-Find to check for cycles and connectivity.
Graph Valid Tree.description: You have a graph of n nodes labeled from 0 to n - 1. You are given an integer n and a list of edges where edges[i] = [ai, bi] indicates that there is an undirected edge between nodes ai and bi in the graph. Return true if the edges of the given graph make up a valid tree, and false otherwise.

Number of Connected Components in an Und.explanation: Use Union-Find or DFS/BFS. For Union-Find, initialize n components. For each edge, union the two nodes. If they belong to different components, decrement the component count.
Number of Connected Components in an Und.description: You have a graph of n nodes. You are given an integer n and an array edges where edges[i] = [ai, bi] indicates that there is an edge between ai and bi in the graph. Return the number of connected components in the graph.

Insert Interval.explanation: Iterate through the intervals. If the current interval ends before the new interval starts, add it to the result. If it starts after the new interval ends, add the new interval and the rest. Otherwise, merge them by updating the new interval's start and end.
Insert Interval.description: You are given an array of non-overlapping intervals intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi] represent the start and the end of the ith interval and intervals is sorted in ascending order by starti. You are also given an interval newInterval = [start, end] that represents the start and end of another interval. Insert newInterval into intervals such that intervals is still sorted in ascending order by starti and intervals still does not have any overlapping intervals (merge overlapping intervals if necessary).

Merge Intervals.explanation: Sort the intervals by their start time. Iterate through them, and if the current interval overlaps with the last merged interval, update the end time of the last merged interval. Otherwise, add the current interval to the result.
Merge Intervals.description: Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], merge all overlapping intervals, and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals that cover all the intervals in the input.

Non-overlapping Intervals.explanation: Sort the intervals by their end time. Iterate through them, keeping track of the end time of the last non-overlapping interval. If the current interval starts before the last end time, it overlaps, so increment the removal count.
Non-overlapping Intervals.description: Given an array of intervals intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], return the minimum number of intervals you need to remove to make the rest of the intervals non-overlapping.

Meeting Rooms.explanation: Sort the intervals by start time. Check if any interval starts before the previous one ends. If so, return False.
Meeting Rooms.description: Given an array of meeting time intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], determine if a person could attend all meetings.

Meeting Rooms II.explanation: Separate start and end times into two sorted arrays. Use two pointers to iterate through them. If a meeting starts before the earliest ending meeting ends, you need a new room. Otherwise, a room frees up.
Meeting Rooms II.description: Given an array of meeting time intervals intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], return the minimum number of conference rooms required.

Reverse Linked List.explanation: Use two pointers, prev (initially None) and curr (initially head). Iterate through the list, saving the next node, reversing the current node's pointer to prev, and then advancing prev and curr.
Reverse Linked List.description: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list, and return the reversed list.

Linked List Cycle.explanation: Use Floyd's Tortoise and Hare algorithm. Have a slow pointer move one step and a fast pointer move two steps. If they ever meet, there is a cycle.
Linked List Cycle.description: Given head, the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle in it.

Merge Two Sorted Lists.explanation: Use a dummy node to simplify edge cases. Compare the heads of the two lists, append the smaller one to the merged list, and advance the corresponding pointer.
Merge Two Sorted Lists.description: You are given the heads of two sorted linked lists list1 and list2. Merge the two lists in a one sorted list. The list should be made by splicing together the nodes of the first two lists.

Merge k Sorted Lists.explanation: Use a min-heap (priority queue) or divide and conquer. For divide and conquer, pair up lists and merge them using the 'Merge Two Sorted Lists' algorithm until only one list remains.
Merge k Sorted Lists.description: You are given an array of k linked-lists lists, each linked-list is sorted in ascending order. Merge all the linked-lists into one sorted linked-list and return it.

Remove Nth Node From End of List.explanation: Use a dummy node and two pointers, left and right. Move the right pointer n steps ahead. Then move both pointers until the right pointer reaches the end. The left pointer will be just before the node to remove.
Remove Nth Node From End of List.description: Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return its head.

Reorder List.explanation: Find the middle of the list using slow and fast pointers. Reverse the second half of the list. Then merge the two halves by alternating nodes.
Reorder List.implementation:
class Solution:
    def reorderList(self, head: Optional[ListNode]) -> None:
        slow, fast = head, head.next
        while fast and fast.next:
            slow = slow.next
            fast = fast.next.next
        second = slow.next
        prev = slow.next = None
        while second:
            tmp = second.next
            second.next = prev
            prev = second
            second = tmp
        first, second = head, prev
        while second:
            tmp1, tmp2 = first.next, second.next
            first.next = second
            second.next = tmp1
            first, second = tmp1, tmp2
Reorder List.description: You are given the head of a singly linked-list. The list can be represented as: L0 → L1 → … → Ln - 1 → Ln. Reorder the list to be on the following form: L0 → Ln → L1 → Ln - 1 → L2 → Ln - 2 → …

Set Matrix Zeroes.explanation: Use the first row and first column as markers to indicate if a row or column should be zeroed. Use a separate variable for the first row/column to avoid overlapping.
Set Matrix Zeroes.description: Given an m x n integer matrix matrix, if an element is 0, set its entire row and column to 0's. You must do it in place.

Spiral Matrix.explanation: Keep track of the boundaries: left, right, top, bottom. Iterate in a spiral pattern (right, down, left, up), shrinking the boundaries after each direction is completed.
Spiral Matrix.description: Given an m x n matrix, return all elements of the matrix in spiral order.

Rotate Image.explanation: Rotate the matrix layer by layer. For each layer, move elements in a 4-way swap. Alternatively, transpose the matrix (swap rows and columns) and then reverse each row.
Rotate Image.description: You are given an n x n 2D matrix representing an image, rotate the image by 90 degrees (clockwise). You have to rotate the image in-place, which means you have to modify the input 2D matrix directly.

Word Search.explanation: Use backtracking (DFS). For each cell, if it matches the first letter of the word, start a DFS. Keep track of visited cells in the current path to avoid reusing them.
Word Search.description: Given an m x n grid of characters board and a string word, return true if word exists in the grid. The word can be constructed from letters of sequentially adjacent cells, where adjacent cells are horizontally or vertically neighboring.

Longest Substring Without Repeating Char.explanation: Use a sliding window with a hash set. Keep adding characters to the set. If a duplicate is found, remove characters from the left until the duplicate is gone. Update the max length at each step.
Longest Substring Without Repeating Char.description: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

Valid Anagram.explanation: Count the frequency of each character in both strings. If the frequencies match for all characters, they are anagrams. You can use a hash map or an array of size 26 for lowercase English letters.
Valid Anagram.description: Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s, and false otherwise.

Valid Parentheses.explanation: Use a stack. Iterate through the string. If it's an opening bracket, push it onto the stack. If it's a closing bracket, check if the stack is not empty and the top of the stack is the corresponding opening bracket. If so, pop it. Otherwise, return False.
Valid Parentheses.description: Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid.

Valid Palindrome.explanation: Use two pointers, one at the beginning and one at the end. Move them towards the center, skipping non-alphanumeric characters. Compare the characters at the pointers (ignoring case). If they don't match, return False.
Valid Palindrome.description: A phrase is a palindrome if, after converting all uppercase letters into lowercase letters and removing all non-alphanumeric characters, it reads the same forward and backward. Alphanumeric characters include letters and numbers. Given a string s, return true if it is a palindrome, or false otherwise.

Maximum Depth of Binary Tree.explanation: Use recursion (DFS). The maximum depth of a tree is 1 plus the maximum depth of its left and right subtrees. Base case is an empty tree (depth 0).
Maximum Depth of Binary Tree.description: Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth. A binary tree's maximum depth is the number of nodes along the longest path from the root node down to the farthest leaf node.

Longest Repeating Character Replacement.explanation: Use a sliding window. Keep a count of character frequencies in the current window. The window is valid if the window length minus the count of the most frequent character is <= k.
Longest Repeating Character Replacement.description: You are given a string s and an integer k. You can choose any character of the string and change it to any other uppercase English character. You can perform this operation at most k times. Return the length of the longest substring containing the same letter you can get after performing the above operations.

Minimum Window Substring.explanation: Use a sliding window and two hash maps (or one). Expand the right pointer to include characters until the window contains all characters of t. Then shrink the left pointer to minimize the window while still containing all characters of t.
Minimum Window Substring.description: Given two strings s and t of lengths m and n respectively, return the minimum window substring of s such that every character in t (including duplicates) is included in the window. If there is no such substring, return the empty string "".

Group Anagrams.explanation: Use a hash map where the key is the character count (as a tuple) and the value is a list of anagrams. Iterate through the strings, compute their character counts, and group them.
Group Anagrams.description: Given an array of strings strs, group the anagrams together. You can return the answer in any order.

Longest Palindromic Substring.explanation: Expand around center. For each character (and each pair of adjacent characters), expand outwards as long as the characters match. Keep track of the longest palindrome found.
Longest Palindromic Substring.description: Given a string s, return the longest palindromic substring in s.

Palindromic Substrings.explanation: Similar to Longest Palindromic Substring, expand around center. For each character and each pair of adjacent characters, expand outwards and count every valid palindrome found.
Palindromic Substrings.description: Given a string s, return the number of palindromic substrings in it. A string is a palindrome when it reads the same backward as forward. A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within the string.

Encode and Decode Strings.explanation: To encode, prefix each string with its length followed by a delimiter (e.g., '5#hello'). To decode, read the length, skip the delimiter, and extract the string.
Encode and Decode Strings.description: Design an algorithm to encode a list of strings to a string. The encoded string is then sent over the network and is decoded back to the original list of strings.

Same Tree.explanation: Use recursion (DFS). If both nodes are null, return True. If one is null or values don't match, return False. Recursively check left and right subtrees.
Same Tree.description: Given the roots of two binary trees p and q, write a function to check if they are the same or not. Two binary trees are considered the same if they are structurally identical, and the nodes have the same value.

Invert Binary Tree.explanation: Use recursion (DFS). Swap the left and right children of the current node, then recursively invert the left and right subtrees.
Invert Binary Tree.implementation:
class Solution:
    def invertTree(self, root: Optional[TreeNode]) -> Optional[TreeNode]:
        if not root: return None
        tmp = root.left
        root.left = root.right
        root.right = tmp
        self.invertTree(root.left)
        self.invertTree(root.right)
        return root
Invert Binary Tree.description: Given the root of a binary tree, invert the tree, and return its root.

Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum.explanation: Use DFS. For each node, compute the max path sum of its left and right subtrees (ignoring negative sums). Update the global max with the path passing through the current node. Return the max path extending from the current node to its parent.
Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum.description: A path in a binary tree is a sequence of nodes where each pair of adjacent nodes in the sequence has an edge connecting them. A node can only appear in the sequence at most once. Note that the path does not need to pass through the root. The path sum of a path is the sum of the node's values in the path. Given the root of a binary tree, return the maximum path sum of any non-empty path.

Binary Tree Level Order Traversal.explanation: Use BFS with a queue. For each level, iterate through the current nodes in the queue, add their values to a level list, and enqueue their children.
Binary Tree Level Order Traversal.description: Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values. (i.e., from left to right, level by level).

Subtree of Another Tree.explanation: Use DFS. For each node in the main tree, check if the subtree starting at that node is identical to the target tree (using the Same Tree logic).
Subtree of Another Tree.description: Given the roots of two binary trees root and subRoot, return true if there is a subtree of root with the same structure and node values of subRoot and false otherwise.

Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and.explanation: The first element in preorder is the root. Find this root in inorder to split the tree into left and right subtrees. Recursively build the left and right subtrees.
Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and.implementation:
class Solution:
    def buildTree(self, preorder: List[int], inorder: List[int]) -> Optional[TreeNode]:
        if not preorder or not inorder: return None
        root = TreeNode(preorder[0])
        mid = inorder.index(preorder[0])
        root.left = self.buildTree(preorder[1:mid + 1], inorder[:mid])
        root.right = self.buildTree(preorder[mid + 1:], inorder[mid + 1:])
        return root
Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and.description: Given two integer arrays preorder and inorder where preorder is the preorder traversal of a binary tree and inorder is the inorder traversal of the same tree, construct and return the binary tree.

Validate Binary Search Tree.explanation: Use DFS. Pass down the valid range (min_val, max_val) for each node. A node's value must be strictly between its min_val and max_val.
Validate Binary Search Tree.description: Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree (BST).

Kth Smallest Element in a BST.explanation: Use inorder traversal (iterative with a stack or recursive). Inorder traversal of a BST visits nodes in ascending order. Keep track of the number of visited nodes and return the kth one.
Kth Smallest Element in a BST.description: Given the root of a binary search tree, and an integer k, return the kth smallest value (1-indexed) of all the values of the nodes in the tree.

Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST.explanation: Use the BST property. If both p and q are greater than the root, the LCA is in the right subtree. If both are less, it's in the left subtree. Otherwise, the current root is the LCA.
Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST.description: Given a binary search tree (BST), find the lowest common ancestor (LCA) node of two given nodes in the BST.

Implement Trie (Prefix Tree).explanation: Use a tree structure where each node represents a character and contains a dictionary of its children and a boolean flag indicating the end of a word.
Implement Trie (Prefix Tree).description: A trie (pronounced as "try") or prefix tree is a tree data structure used to efficiently store and retrieve keys in a dataset of strings. There are various applications of this very efficient data structure, such as autocomplete and spellchecker. Implement the Trie class.

Top K Frequent Elements.explanation: Count frequencies using a hash map. Then use bucket sort: create an array of lists where the index is the frequency. Iterate backwards through the array to get the top k elements.
Top K Frequent Elements.description: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequent elements. You may return the answer in any order.

Find Median from Data Stream.explanation: Use two heaps: a max-heap for the smaller half of numbers and a min-heap for the larger half. Keep their sizes balanced. The median is either the top of the larger heap or the average of both tops.
Find Median from Data Stream.description: The median is the middle value in an ordered integer list. If the size of the list is even, there is no middle value, and the median is the mean of the two middle values. Implement the MedianFinder class.

Search a 2D Matrix.explanation: Treat the 2D matrix as a 1D sorted array and use binary search. Convert 1D index to 2D coordinates using division and modulo by the number of columns.
Search a 2D Matrix.implementation:
class Solution:
    def searchMatrix(self, matrix: List[List[int]], target: int) -> bool:
        ROWS, COLS = len(matrix), len(matrix[0])
        top, bot = 0, ROWS - 1
        while top <= bot:
            row = (top + bot) // 2
            if target > matrix[row][-1]: top = row + 1
            elif target < matrix[row][0]: bot = row - 1
            else: break
        if not (top <= bot): return False
        row = (top + bot) // 2
        l, r = 0, COLS - 1
        while l <= r:
            m = (l + r) // 2
            if target > matrix[row][m]: l = m + 1
            elif target < matrix[row][m]: r = m - 1
            else: return True
        return False
Search a 2D Matrix.description: You are given an m x n integer matrix matrix with the following two properties: Each row is sorted in non-decreasing order. The first integer of each row is greater than the last integer of the previous row. Given an integer target, return true if target is in matrix or false otherwise.

Koko Eating Bananas.explanation: Use binary search on the eating speed (k). The minimum speed is 1, and the maximum is the largest pile. For each speed, calculate the total hours needed. If it's <= h, try a slower speed.
Koko Eating Bananas.description: Koko loves to eat bananas. There are n piles of bananas, the ith pile has piles[i] bananas. The guards have gone and will come back in h hours. Koko can decide her bananas-per-hour eating speed of k. Each hour, she chooses some pile of bananas and eats k bananas from that pile. If the pile has less than k bananas, she eats all of them instead and will not eat any more bananas during this hour. Return the minimum integer k such that she can eat all the bananas within h hours.

Time Based Key-Value Store.explanation: Use a hash map where the key maps to a list of (value, timestamp) pairs. Since timestamps are strictly increasing, use binary search to find the largest timestamp <= the target timestamp.
Time Based Key-Value Store.description: Design a time-based key-value data structure that can store multiple values for the same key at different time stamps and retrieve the key's value at a certain timestamp. Implement the TimeMap class.

Binary Search.explanation: Standard binary search. Maintain left and right pointers. Calculate mid. If target is greater than mid, search right half. If less, search left half.

Min Stack.explanation: Use two stacks. One stack stores the actual values, and the other stack stores the minimum value seen so far up to that point.
Min Stack.description: Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving the minimum element in constant time. Implement the MinStack class.

Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation.explanation: Use a stack. Iterate through the tokens. If it's a number, push it. If it's an operator, pop two numbers, apply the operator, and push the result back.
Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation.description: You are given an array of strings tokens that represents an arithmetic expression in a Reverse Polish Notation. Evaluate the expression. Return an integer that represents the value of the expression.

Generate Parentheses.explanation: Use backtracking. Keep track of the number of open and closed parentheses used. Add an open parenthesis if open < n. Add a closed parenthesis if closed < open.
Generate Parentheses.description: Given n pairs of parentheses, write a function to generate all combinations of well-formed parentheses.

Daily Temperatures.explanation: Use a monotonic decreasing stack. Store pairs of (temperature, index). If the current temperature is greater than the top of the stack, pop the stack and calculate the difference in indices.
Daily Temperatures.description: Given an array of integers temperatures represents the daily temperatures, return an array answer such that answer[i] is the number of days you have to wait after the ith day to get a warmer temperature. If there is no future day for which this is possible, keep answer[i] == 0 instead.

Car Fleet.explanation: Sort cars by starting position in descending order. Calculate the time it takes for each car to reach the target. If a car takes less or equal time than the car ahead of it, it joins its fleet (so don't add it to the stack).
Car Fleet.description: There are n cars going to the same destination along a one-lane road. The destination is target miles away. You are given two integer array position and speed, both of length n, where position[i] is the position of the ith car and speed[i] is the speed of the ith car (in miles per hour). Return the number of car fleets that will arrive at the destination.

Kth Largest Element in an Array.explanation: Use QuickSelect for O(n) average time, or a min-heap of size k for O(n log k) time. The heap approach is simpler: push elements, and if size > k, pop the smallest.
Kth Largest Element in an Array.description: Description for Kth Largest Element in an Array

Task Scheduler.explanation: Use a max-heap to keep track of task frequencies, and a queue to keep track of tasks that are cooling down (storing [count, available_time]).
